sentences.text: Well, the Thebbnz were like that, T-H-E-B-B-N-Z, Shultz, S-L-U-- no.
S-H-U-L-T-Z.
Shultz.
Somehow this were shops.
They were German shops.
They were German firms who were assigned-- they probably got permission from the German, from the Nazi government-- to stage the shops and a lot, a lot of money.
And have the Jews laborers, slave laborers, to stitch uniforms for the Germans.
Wehrmacht.
[COUGHS]
So we got an announcement one day that we should move into the premises and leave our own flat in 26 [GERMAN] Street.

And my father said us, to my stepmother and me, and all the other people who lived there, but predominantly us, to bring-- we still had, you know, like in every Jewish home, there was a lot of beautiful crockery from [?
Carlsbad, ?] from Marienbad, from Vienna.
God knows, you know.
And he succeeded to get the big ladder in the little kitchen.
The kitchen was there.

And we had to bring, my stepmother and I, from the cupboards wherever we kept, crammed these things, and to give it to him, each, you know, services of crockery, of beautiful you know, sets. 12, 12, 12.
And from the top of the ladder, he slammed it down to the floor, so that it should be broken to pieces.
He didn't want to leave it for the Germans, or for the Poles, or for anybody.
Because the minute we left this flat in [?
Fada ?] and were incarcerated in Schultz Thebbnz shops, we knew there would be no return.
So every single bit of this beautiful crockery was broken.
Up, he was standing there, and [?
I-- ?] and dropped to the floor.
And every other thing, whatever we could damage, we damaged.
And a curious thing happened.
While he was on the ladder down-- and my face must have been-- like, looked like, you know, Christ looking at the crucifixion, full of pain.
He bent down to me and he, in a curious way, he said-- in a very pleasant manner, darling, or something-- you will survive the war.
Don't worry, he said to me.
You will survive the war.
I never forget it.
And I never thought, in a million years, that such a thing could happen.
But that he felt, and that he told me.
And we destroyed, we destroyed whatever-- we couldn't destroy everything.
And anyway, we already lost many things, and we sold so much.
But whatever was breakable, we broke.
We moved into this Thebbnz [? shop ?] premises, and they were not terribly bad.
We were placed with a few other people.
But it was not too bad, I must say.
And immediately, we were shown our-- had to go down, premises were up.
We went down.
Actually, the [?
Schubert's ?] Thebbnz Schultz shop in the small ghetto where I was was located in a very famous, and very prodigious, and very, very excellent Jewish school called Vavelberg.
It was a boys' high school and very famous school in Warsaw-- Vavelberg.
And what they did, they took all the writing desks, all the desks for students and pupils, and cramped it down.
It was a vast place with a big, empty space.
So the building was great-- enormous building.
And then, in the corner of this empty space-- which was a vast place-- they crammed all the, all the writing tables for the students.
Now, we set to work.
We were stitching.
There was very little food given.
You had to work hard from morning to late.
And then was soup given, bread ration.
You know, very, very frugal.
Frugal is the-- it's an, it's an excellent word, you know, which is far above that.
And we started to work, and we stitched the uniforms.
It was designed one did this, one did that, one did this.
I enlisted my mother-- my Rosa, my stepmother.
And she did-- she, she collected things from the stitching and put in the room.
She was busy, though.
Because she was busy, she could get food.
And my father was left at-- in the flat, because he couldn't get work anymore.
He was not older than 55 at that time.
And the little boy of a nice young woman who lost her husband-- and she was placed with us.
The little boy-- my-- and we used to bring whatever we couldn't manage to eat, you know?
And we didn't want to eat, we brought up.
And my father used to, you know, to, to cook for the little boy, take care of the little boy, like a grandfather.
Made out of matches, made him little trains and things like that.
And we were happy that my father is there.
Now, one day, shortly after we made-- we went there, the Germans told us to go down, not to take anything.
We were just going to go for a-- for a-- to see something, to another-- to, to, to this other ghetto to see-- they told us, to see how they worked there, or some flimsy excuse-- about 500 of us.
And I-- when my mother, my stepmother, when I saw-- because she was always removing the stitched-on things into another room-- I said to her-- made the sign that she should go.
Because why should she go  for the selection?
I didn't know what it was, yet I had to go down.
There was no way.
They encircled us.
They-- with soldiers and guns and so on.
It was always, always very forbidding and frightening.
And I made the sign that she should go back.
So she went back.
I went down.
We were about 500.
As it happens-- so it was like that-- on the left hand, let's see-- on the right-hand side-- I still remember it vividly-- there were soldiers with rifles.
On the left-hand side were Jewish policeman.
And as it happens, next to me, when I went down-- we were about 500 strong-- there was a Jewish policeman, and his sister was next to me.
Because we, in this Thebbnz workshop, there were masses of people because they were in an enormous place.
And he, as we were standing there, just beginning to march, he said, run away, run away, run away.
And as I heard it, and fearing him--
To his sister.
I was standing next to the sister.
And she-- he said it to me, probably, too.
As, as, as I heard this-- and I was already-- a premonition in my heart was there all the time, the fear.
I-- without any, you know, thought, I began to run towards this benches-- which I explained to you-- chairs and benches at the end of the place.
And this girl, another maybe three, four people, in full speed-- but it was, look, that the soldiers on the other side didn't see us and didn't shoot us outright which they did-- it was a miracle.
So we run into this place-- it was quite far-- hid there.
And they were still amassing people from the workshop into the big queue to take them probably straight to Umschlagplatz and Treblinka.
And this Jewish policeman already knew the secret.
We still didn't realize what was happening, but had a fear-- noises, some news were getting through to us.
Anyway, we ran there.
And the whole lot of nearly 500 people-- women, men, young, some women took the children with them--
And we, we hid there.
It was very early at that time.
Maybe, maybe it was 12 o'clock, early-- 11:00.
And we-- the whole day, we were sitting in these benches.
How can I tell you?
We, we couldn't stretch our legs.
It was crammed.
We were hungry.
It was, it was terrible.
But we communicated with each other by talking to each other, you know?
Mhm.
And we didn't move out of this, of the fear, that they might see-- the Germans might see us.
So we stayed there till we heard the noises, about 8:00 in the evening, about-- that, that they returned.
And nice voices, very jolly.
They went to drink, the Germans, full of fun.
So we crawled out and went to our places-- where we, we could find our places.
And I found, I found my-- I, I found my stepmother waiting for me, and my father was already taken.
We, we didn't even see him.
He was taken with the little boy in his arms.
And when, when we came to the flat, we could see still the soup my stepmother-- the soup was still warm he was cooking for him.
And the [NON-ENGLISH] the soup, and all the little train, the little rag toys, which we made for the little boy, were spread all over the place.
And obviously, the German entered, [NON-ENGLISH].
It was that-- in one moment, my, my stepmother was a widow.
I was without a father.
She was without her husband.
And that poor woman was without the son.
And so this was the first selection.
Then I, I couldn't go back to the stitching anymore.
So I befriended somebody who worked in the kitchens.
Because this ticket was somehow torn--
I couldn't even remember it.
And we-- I had to-- oh, I thought it safer-- or more food, more food, probably because of more-- I befriended somebody who worked in the kitchen.
He was a very nice man, actually.
He was the boss.
And we went with my stepmother to work in the kitchens.
Of course, you can imagine the-- we had sore hands from cutting vegetables.
Mind you, there were three kitchens.
One kitchen was for the Germans.
And they had exquisite food cooked for them by experts.
We cleaned the vegetables and cleaned the kitchen.
And there was the food for all of us, which was rubbish, rotten vegetable soup, cabbage.
Cabbage, you know, I can't even eat cabbage today.
And then was the food for themselves, for the staff.
And we helped and worked there.
And already at that time, the manager of the kitchen, the Jew, he used to say to us that he fears that this ghetto will be liquidated.
And therefore, he's building, with some other people-- but he can't tell us, naturally-- a bunker.
That was quite-- I, I would say, this-- it was-- it was-- I heard often about-- the Jewish people built bunkers.
Bunkers means shelters [INAUDIBLE].
And they provided that they put food there, they put whatever, at the time, was still possible, and beddings and things.
And they made it deep into the earth.
Well, we had no access to such a place.
No way.
And we worked in the kitchens.
And then one day was another selection, after, after a few weeks or something.
It was another few weeks or-- eventually, a month or two, was another selection.
So they-- we were told to go [INAUDIBLE].
I knew where that-- my stepmother, couldn't-- I couldn't let her go.
So I said, no, run.
We go run upstairs.
So we run upstairs-- run upstairs, we didn't know where.
We run upstairs to a dormitory.
And then I heard the German-- I heard the Jewish policeman calling, calling.
Anyway, I had a brainwave.
I tore open a pillow and spread all the feathers--
On my stepmother and pushed her down, all the feathers on her so that she's not visible.
And as I wanted to hide myself, I couldn't because the German police-- the Jewish policemen was up and said, you must go down.
You must go down.
You must go down.
So he didn't allow me to hide.
I went down.
So I-- my stepmother was safe, and I went down.
And as I went down, you know, they, they allowed us to go to a toilet, I think.
I don't know.
I must have had a slip or some sort of lipstick, I don't know what-- or a piece of comb that, after the work we used to-- and I didn't know, should I put the lipstick?
Should I not put the lipstick?
Anyway, I combed my hair.
I the sort of a shade of the lipstick.
And I went down and joined the queue.
Now, It was an enormous queue.
And I already knew, felt, that after-- I didn't know where, I didn't know what was being done to the Jews.
Nobody knew.
But it was feeling, I was feeling that it's nothing good coming of it.
And I didn't know how to defend myself.
Nobody knew how to defend themselves.
And as we were staying in this queue, along came from far-- we have seen approaching that queue, two young men, very good-looking, what the-- Germans with the Aryan look, you know?
Blond with blue eyes, dressed in these fabulous leather coats, with two enormous dogs.
And approaching each of-- everybody in the queue.
You to the left.
You to the right.
And you to the left, and you to the right.
And afterwards, they'll say, you, is it left, right, left, right, left, right.
When he came to us, I was standing with a friend of mine, with a girl who I worked with.
And he said to her, you go left, to me, you go right.
I didn't know whether right is good or left is good.
As it happened, right was good.

So the left, left, all the left-- you see off a whim, off a whim, off an arrogant, absolutely arrogant, probably stupid boy with two fierce dogs, he was the-- our God almighty to tell us whether we should live or die.
That, that's how it was, you see?
So I, I survived.
She went to the left.
I went to the right.
All that enormous queue, I can tell you, 3/4 of it.
He-- what they choose was an able-body working force to make them-- you know, to see that they slowly would die of starvation.
But before they die, they should work.
And those he didn't particularly-- it was random.
It was at random.
It was nothing to-- no rhyme to it, nothing, left, right, left, right.
And they all were taken straight away to Umschlagplatz--
The others-- and straight away to Treblinka.
And I--
But what kind of-- I mean, it was real random.
Nothing, no rhyme to--
Certainly the younger they preserved, I don't know even if good-looking ones.
The young, they preserved.
And the elder, straightaway off.
So that was the second selection of mine.
Then I knew, you see-- then I could go back.
And I found my stepmother.
Poor girl, she looked like a feather, like a bird from the paradise somewhere-- unknown country-- full of feathers still in her hair and all that.
And then I knew already then that that is nearing, you know, to then.
And somehow, shortly afterwards, I met in the ghetto, one of my great friends, who was already at the university.
And we were a big, like you say, [NON-ENGLISH].
You see, we all had this, like you say, [HEBREW].
[HEBREW], like you say in Hebrew.
It was a group, and he was one of those.
He actually married one of these girls from our group.
And I said-- he looked so handsome.
He looked so well-dressed.
I was surprised.
I said, Michael, what are you doing?
Of course, all in Polish.
He said, look, come somewhere.
I have got here a room where I'm hiding.
And I'll tell you.
So he took me to his place.
It was somewhere in the underground, some ruin, and so on.
And he said that he's working for the underground.
It was a Polish-Jewish underground.
Outside the ghetto.
The Polish people were also helping.
There was a big organization, a Polish organization, to which my cousin belonged to, also helped the Jews.
And he's trying to get out as many Jews out of the ghetto as he can.
They are bribing the, the gate-- at the gate.
And it-- and he smuggles them out, the Jewish people.
Not through canals, not through anything, like some people went through canals.
Just out of the gate, he smuggles as many as he can.
Because he said, look, this ghetto is going to be liquidated.
Can't you think of somebody you, you-- and he's-- he was-- has to be dressed like either a Pole or like a German.
He had an excellent good look.
It's all nonsense about Jewish people looking like, like, God knows who.
They were-- even those who had [NON-ENGLISH], when you would shave the [NON-ENGLISH] off, then they looked beautiful and handsome.
But-- so he said he is working for this underground.
He's trying to save.
And what am I doing in the ghetto?
I said, I am with my stepmother.
I have no way-- other way.
And what happens to others will happen to me.
He says, nonsense.
You are young.
You have to save yourself.
How?
He, he, he said, do you remember-- do you have anybody outside the ghetto?
Then I remembered I had a cousin who married a non-Jewish girl, a Christian girl, before the war.
I knew his address, even.
I said, I don't know if he'll recognize me.
I don't know if he will want to help me.
And he said, look, you have to try.
And if you don't succeed with him, I'll provide you with-- you have to memorize-- I usually put in the hem of my skirt a piece, a slip of paper, to memorize the telephone number which I can contact.
Because what happens-- his father already set up the organization.
It was outside Warsaw.
And he was communicating.
So if, if I didn't have-- if I wouldn't succeed to find my cousin, he would, he would provide me with shelter.
But I said, I have my stepmother here.
He said, try.
Maybe he will take you both out.
Well, I went to, to our place, to the kitchen where we worked, and I told Rosa about it.
And she said, you know, it might be an idea-- like that is sure something terrible is going to happen-- maybe.
So anyway, one day he organized it, a few other people, too.
And I was shivering with fright, you can well imagine.
And he organized-- and we went out of the ghetto.
Only you?
No, no.
A few other people.
But we were not--
No, no, no, no.
No, no, no, no.
First I-- first I had to investigate what can be done.
You understand?
So it was quite traumatic to go to the gate and to wait, and, and maybe he would shoot you, you know?
Because nothing was sure.
But I went out.
And he said in a few weeks time, he would be back, that he will know.
If I contacted him or whatever, or if I didn't succeed, then I have to let him know.
Anyway, I went out of the ghetto.
And the first impression was dreadful, gray.
It was gray.
It was cold.
It was unpleasant.
It was almost the end of '42, because the selections were taking place.
I didn't know anything which was happening in the big ghetto.
But there-- our small ghetto was-- the selections were all the time.
But when you went outside, it was a grim view.
[PHONE RINGS] Don't take any notice.
And they said always-- [PHONE RINGS] Say I'm not at home.
It was all the time were big placards for-- unless it's my daughter, if it's my daughter.
It was written, verboten, verboten, verboten.
Forbidden, forbidden.
Big, big, big placards and saying-- this is verboten, verboten, always.
And gray and poor-looking, and sort of, you know, the trams.
You were struck by extreme poverty and foreboding feeling of-- all unpleasant.
I made my way-- I think it was early, very early-- and I made to my way to this street.
It, I think-- there was [NON-ENGLISH] Street-- it was called Golden Street-- [NON-ENGLISH].
I was given by my friend [NON-ENGLISH] you know, to keep with me.
Money.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And I could go to a little shop and buy myself a bun.
But for fear of being recognized or something like that, I didn't.
Anyway, eventually, like midday, slowly, slowly-- I couldn't recognize-- because it was-- the walls were the whole-- my own orientation, I couldn't orientate myself.
I was disorientated, which way to go, how to find the place.
And I was afraid to approach anybody.
Where is the place?
Eventually I found it.
I asked somebody who was on the street, looked, looked a poor man or something.
I was not afraid of him, and he directed me.
When I went into this building-- it was on the second floor or something-- I rang the door.
And I-- a man opened the-- and saw me.
And I said, I'm looking for my, my, my-- it-- his name was Rubin also.
It was Mieczyslaw Rubin.
He slammed the door at once.
We don't know him.
We don't want to know anything about him.
But I recognized these people.
I recognized these people because at some point of life, I met them before.
I knew they were friends of my cousin.
I think they were also associated with this electric shop which my, my little brother was involved-- worked there for a time.
And I said to myself, I have to find out where is my cousin.
I have no other way.
So I knocked on the door again.
I said, look, I'm going to be here on the stairs as long-- till you give me his address.
I won't move.
And I can't tell you how many hours, till it was dark.
Slammed the door, he didn't open.
And I-- each time I knocked there, I said, I'm still here.
It was already quite dark.
Maybe not at midnight, but dark-- you know, it was already autumn or something, like the beginning of autumn, maybe September or something.
It was not yet dark.
But by the time it got dark, very dark, suddenly, a little piece of paper was slipped through the door.
And it was written, Mieczyslaw Rubinski he was-- his name-- lives here, and here, and here.
Go away.
They were afraid because if somebody would find me on this step, they were in-- endangered.
So they gave me the address.
Then now, it was another odyssey how to find my cousin.
It was a completely new address, a different address.
But anyway, to cut the story short, I did find him.
I found the address.
He opened the door.
It was my cousin.
And I simply fell into his arms.
And he was bewildered, completely bewildered, and let me in, of course.
And his wife was a very pretty Polish girl.
And they had a little girl.
And they told me-- they could-- somehow, they couldn't have children, so they adopted the little girl.
And I told him the story.
And he was very kind.
And he said, now, come.
And they let me-- they let me have-- wash myself.
They gave me something to eat.
And I told him my story.
And I said-- and I was delighted to find him.
And I told him how difficult it was to find him.
Yes, he said.
They were given this flat-- sold the flat to this friends.
But they actually forbid them to tell the address.
So they probably communicated with him by telephone, and so on.
And he said, yes, because I give my name, and so on.
So they-- he already knew I would be coming.
And-- well, we sat late into the night, and I related to him what has happened.
He told me that he is actually hiding his own mother.
He's hiding his own mother in a place called Zielonka with his wife's far relative.
He can take me because I can, I can present myself as the nanny to the little girl, to the little daughter.
But he cannot take my stepmother because he has no room for her.
He is simply afraid.
There is no way he can take her.
So I was heartbroken, you know, absolutely heartbroken.
So I said, look, I think whatever happens to her will happen to me.
They didn't allow me to go.
I was there for about a week or something.
And I didn't even let my friend know what is happening to me.
I was afraid to telephone.
I didn't want to endanger them.
And, and he-- and, and I decided to go back to the ghetto.
